Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to dedicate around 25 hours a week, but it was scattered—some days I'd study for hours, other days I couldn't focus at all. I learn best by listening, so I'd often play lecture recordings while doing other things, which helped me absorb information without feeling overwhelmed. The anxiety made it hard to stick to any consistent schedule because I'd get paralyzed thinking about everything I needed to do.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how stressed I was feeling. When deadlines approached, I'd cram intensely, but during quieter weeks I'd sometimes avoid studying altogether because the thought of it made me anxious. I kept telling myself I'd establish a regular schedule, but between my extracurriculars and this constant fear of falling behind, it never really happened. Weekends were usually my catch-up time when I felt less pressure.
Q3: Usually it was panic about an upcoming deadline or realizing I'd missed something important. Sometimes a peer would message me asking about an assignment, and that would remind me to check in. I also logged in when I received notification emails, though I'll admit I often let those pile up before actually acting on them. Fear was honestly my biggest motivator—fear of failing, fear of missing something crucial.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There were brief periods where I'd check in daily, but it never became automatic for me. The anxiety I felt about the course made logging in feel more like a chore than a habit. I'd sometimes avoid the LMS for days because seeing my incomplete tasks stressed me out too much. The peer support group tried to help me establish routines, but my engagement stayed pretty inconsistent.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly valuable for me since I'm an auditory learner—I watched over four hours of content and would replay sections multiple times. The discussion forums were also helpful because seeing other students' questions made me realize I wasn't alone in my confusion. When peers explained concepts in their own words, it often clicked better than the original material. I also appreciated any audio-based resources or podcasts the instructors provided.
Q6: I barely touched the reading materials and written assignments, which is reflected in my low completion rate. Reading dense text triggers my anxiety because I feel like I'm not retaining anything. I also ignored a lot of the supplementary resources because I was already overwhelmed with the core content. The discussion posts I made were minimal compared to what was expected—I just couldn't bring myself to engage more actively when I was already scared about my performance.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly helped me understand something I'd be tested on, or if it reduced my anxiety about the course. Listening to explanations—whether from videos, peers, or recordings—always felt productive. If something was confusing and had no clear payoff, I'd abandon it quickly because my stress levels couldn't handle wasting time on things that didn't seem immediately useful.
Q8: Required activities felt like weights on my chest—I knew I had to do them, but that pressure made them harder to start. Optional activities were easier to ignore, which was both a relief and a problem because I missed out on things that could have helped me. I'd tell myself I'd get to the optional stuff after finishing requirements, but that rarely happened. The anxiety around required work often spilled over and made everything feel mandatory and overwhelming.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone during commutes or while exercising. I also had study sessions with peers from my support group where we'd discuss concepts verbally—none of that showed up in the LMS. I used external YouTube videos and podcasts to supplement the course material, especially when the official resources weren't clicking for me.
Q10: There were many times I was genuinely learning but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd listen to downloaded lectures repeatedly, sometimes three or four times, but only the initial download would register. My peer support sessions involved hours of discussion and explanation that happened completely offline. I also spent time just thinking through problems while walking or lying in bed—processing information in my head without any digital trace.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video once, download it or take notes while listening, and then never open it again on the platform. My notes from those sessions became my primary study material. I preferred having offline access because it meant I could study without the anxiety of being connected to the course and seeing all my incomplete tasks. The LMS became more of a content delivery system than a place I actively studied.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety peaked when multiple deadlines converged, and I basically froze. I couldn't bring myself to engage with anything because everything felt equally urgent and impossible. There were also personal things happening—family stress, sleep problems from the anxiety—that made it harder to focus. The peer support helped a bit, but there were weeks where I just couldn't show up mentally.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without actually doing anything productive. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars pulled my attention away, and the confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely. My stress levels directly correlated with how much I avoided the platform—the more stressed I was, the less I engaged, which created this terrible cycle.
Q14: Many times. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. There were nights I sat in front of my computer for hours with course materials open but absorbed almost nothing because my anxiety was too loud. The LMS would show activity, but my brain wasn't really there.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The fear of doing them poorly was somehow worse than the fear of not doing them at all—at least that's how my anxious brain processed it. I'd tell myself I needed to be in the right headspace to do good work, but that headspace rarely came. By the time I forced myself to start, I was usually rushing and producing lower quality work than I was capable of.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that really got me moving. I'd have these intense bursts of activity right before something was due, logging in repeatedly, watching videos at 2x speed, frantically posting in discussions. Without deadlines, I'd drift and avoid the course entirely. It wasn't healthy—the deadline-driven panic added to my stress—but it was the only system that worked for me this semester.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. Good feedback motivated me briefly, but negative feedback, even constructive criticism, would send me into an anxiety spiral. I'd sometimes avoid checking my grades for days because I was scared of what I'd see. When I did receive encouraging feedback, it helped me engage more with that particular topic or instructor. But overall, the fear of negative feedback often outweighed the potential benefit of positive feedback.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming because there were so many components to track across 12 different online courses. I appreciated when modules were clearly organized with audio components, but I struggled when the structure assumed I'd engage with written materials first. The peer support intervention helped because it gave me external structure and accountability that the course itself couldn't provide.
Q19: Having video content was essential for my learning style—that made engagement much easier. But the sheer volume of resources and the way everything was spread across different sections made it harder to know where to focus. I wished there was more guidance on prioritization, like 'if you can only do one thing, do this.' The discussion requirements also felt forced sometimes, which made them feel like busywork rather than genuine engagement opportunities.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. High activity can mean someone is anxious and checking in constantly without actually producing work. It could be someone who's confused and searching for answers they never find. Or someone like me who engages with content but freezes when it comes to actually submitting things because the fear of judgment is too strong. Activity doesn't equal productivity or completion.
Q21: That resonates with me too, in a way. My exam score was decent even though my assignment completion was terrible and my LMS engagement was low. I studied a lot offline—listening to recordings, discussing with peers, processing information in ways that didn't show up digitally. Some students are just efficient or learn through methods that aren't captured by clicks and logins. The LMS doesn't see everything.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, how long they spend on pages. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if I'm actually learning or just letting a video play while I panic. They miss all the offline studying, the peer conversations, the mental processing. They especially miss the emotional experience—the fear, the avoidance, the anxiety that shapes everything I do.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just getting through it. It means my brain is present and actively processing information, not just going through motions. For me, real engagement happens when I'm listening to something that clicks, when a peer explains a concept and I suddenly get it, when I'm curious rather than scared. It's about connection to the content, not just exposure to it.
Q24: The LMS would never show how scared I was throughout this entire course. It wouldn't show the hours I spent frozen with anxiety, unable to start tasks I knew I needed to do. It wouldn't capture the peer support conversations that kept me from giving up entirely, or the offline listening that was my primary learning method. Most importantly, it wouldn't show that despite my low visible engagement, I was fighting hard just to stay in the game.
